metadata.title: Incorporation of the Fasting Plasma FFA Concentration into QUICKI Improves Its Association with Insulin Sensitivity in Nonobese Individuals
metadata.journal: The Journal of Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp study in healthy nonobese individuals with or without first-degree relatives affected by type 2 diabetes mellitus
metadata.demographics: 57 young, healthy, nonobese subjects (38F/19M), age 19-45 years, white race, body mass index less than 27 kg/m2, sedentary lifestyle, no history of hypertension, endocrine/metabolic disease, or cigarette smoking

evidence.quote: TheJournalofClinicalEndocrinology&Metabolism86(10):4776–4781 PrintedinU.S.A. Copyright©2001byTheEndocrineSociety
Incorporation of the Fasting Plasma FFA Concentration into QUICKI Improves Its Association with Insulin Sensitivity in Nonobese Individuals
GIANLUCA PERSEGHIN, ANDREA CAUMO, MARIANNA CALONI, GIULIO TESTOLIN, AND LIVIO LUZI

Fifty-sevenhealthy,young,nonobesesubjectswererecruitedattheIstitutoScientificoHSanRaffaele.Themaincriteriafortheirinclusion inthestudywerethefollowing:1)age(19–45yr);2)whiterace;3)body massindexlessthan27kg/m2;4)sedentarylifestyle;and5)nohistory of hypertension, endocrine/metabolic disease, or cigarette smoking.
weperformedaeuglycemichyperinsulinemic clamp[40mU/(m2·min)]in57young,healthy,nonobeseindi vidualswith(n=17)orwithout(n=40)first-degreerelatives
we performed a euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp [40mU/(m2·min)] in 57 young, healthy, nonobese individuals

methods_participants.label: Study population - With family history of type 2 diabetes

evidence.quote: 57 young, healthy, nonobese individuals with (n = 17) or without (n = 40) first-degree relatives affected by type 2 diabetes

methods_participants.label: Study population - Without family history of type 2 diabetes

evidence.quote: 57 young, healthy, nonobese individuals with (n = 17) or without (n = 40) first-degree relatives affected by type 2 diabetes
a subgroup of 14 subjects (age = 35 ± 3 yr, body mass index = 24.5 ± 1.1 kg/m2) performed a dose-response clamp study
The main criteria for their inclusion in the study were the following: 1) age (19–45 yr)
body mass index less than 27 kg/m2
2) white race
Body weight was stable for at least 6–12 months.

methods_participants.label: Euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp - Insulin infusion rate

evidence.quote: euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp [40mU/(m2·min)]

methods_participants.label: Euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp - Target plasma insulin concentration

evidence.quote: Insulin was infused at 40 mU/[m2·min] to reach a plasma insulin concentration of about 350 pmol/liter

methods_participants.label: Euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp - Target plasma glucose concentration

evidence.quote: plasma glucose concentration was kept at 5 mmol/liter for 150 min
plasma glucose concentration was kept at 5 mmol/liter for 150 min

methods_participants.label: Euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp - Blood sampling frequency

evidence.quote: Blood samples for plasma insulin, glucose, and FFAs were drawn every 15 min throughout the study.

methods_participants.label: Dose-response study - Low dose insulin infusion rate

evidence.quote: a dose-response clamp study using insulin infusion rates of 10 (low dose) and 40 (high dose) mU/(m2·min)

methods_participants.label: Dose-response study - High dose insulin infusion rate

evidence.quote: a dose-response clamp study using insulin infusion rates of 10 (low dose) and 40 (high dose) mU/(m2·min)

methods_participants.label: Dose-response study - Low dose target plasma insulin concentration

evidence.quote: The low-dose insulin clamp was designed to reach appropriate (150 pmol/liter) plasma insulin levels
Subjects were studied by means of the euglycemic-hyperinsulinemic clamp to assess insulin sensitivity after a 10-hour overnight fast period.
Subjects were instructed to consume an isocaloric diet and to abstain from exercise activity for 3 wk before the studies.
Women were studied between days 3 and 8 of the menstrual cycle.

Plasma glucose was measured with a glucose analyzer (Beckman Coulter, Inc., Fullerton, CA) and coefficient of variation (CV) was 1.7 ± 0.1% and 3.0 ± 0.4%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.
coefficient of variation (CV) was 1.7 ± 0.1% and 3.0 ± 0.4%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.

Plasma insulin was measured with a microparticle enzyme immunoassay technology with no cross-reactions with proinsulin, c-peptide, and glucagon (IMx insulin assay, Abbott Laboratories, Rome, Italy). CV was 7.2 ± 1.7% and 5.7 ± 0.7%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.
CV was 7.2 ± 1.7% and 5.7 ± 0.7%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.
CV was 5.9 ± 1.8% and 9.3 ± 3.8%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.
CV was 5.9 ± 1.8% and 9.3 ± 3.8%, respectively, in the fasting and clamp conditions.
Plasma was then frozen and stored at −70°C, and FFA determinations were performed as previously described

methods_participants.label: FFA sample storage - Time to analysis

evidence.quote: FFA determinations were performed as previously described within 0–5 working days.
The steady-state period of the insulin clamp was defined as the final 30-min period (i.e. 120–150 min)
The steady-state period of the insulin clamp was defined as the final 30-min period (i.e. 120–150 min)

methods_participants.label: Steady-state clamp - Coefficient of variation threshold

evidence.quote: during which the CV for blood glucose, plasma insulin, and glucose infusion rate (GIR) was less than 7%

narrative_results.statement: S I(clamp), M value, and GIR were inversely associated with fasting FFA

evidence.quote: S I(clamp) (r (cid:1) -0.40, P (cid:7) 0.01), M value (r (cid:1) -0.43, P (cid:7) 0.008), and GIR (r (cid:1) -0.58, P (cid:7) 0.0001) were inversely associated with fasting FFA

narrative_results.statement: HOMA-IR was weakly associated with S I(clamp) and the relationship improved significantly when fasting FFA was incorporated
narrative_results.relationship: weak association improved by FFA incorporation

evidence.quote: HOMA-IR was weakly associated with S I(clamp) (r (cid:1) 0.23, P (cid:1) 0.09). The relationship improved significantly when fasting FFA was incorporated (r (cid:1) 0.47, P (cid:1) 0.0002)

narrative_results.statement: The revised QUICKI markedly improved relationships with both S I(clamp) and GIR

evidence.quote: The use of the revised QUICKI markedly improved the relationships with both S I(clamp) (Fig. 1, right panel: r (cid:1) 0.51, P (cid:7) 0.0001) and GIR (r (cid:1) 0.62, P (cid:7) 0.0001)

narrative_results.statement: Fasting plasma glucose and FFA concentrations were significantly increased in offspring of type 2 diabetic parents compared to normal subjects

evidence.quote: Fasting plasma glucose and FFA concentrations were significantly increased in the offspring of type 2 diabetic parents with respect to the normal subjects

narrative_results.statement: Impaired insulin sensitivity in offspring of type 2 diabetic parents was reflected by 21% and 20% reduction in GIR and S I(clamp) respectively

evidence.quote: Impaired insulin sensitivity in the offspring of type 2 diabetic parents was reflected by 21% and 20% reduction in GIR and S I(clamp), respectively, with respect to normal subjects

narrative_results.statement: HOMA-IR and QUICKI did not detect significant differences in insulin sensitivity between offspring and controls, but revised QUICKI found a significant 10% reduction

evidence.quote: Whereas HOMA-IR and QUICKI did not detect any significant difference in insulin sensitivity between the two groups, the revised QUICKI found a significant reduction in insulin sensitivity in the offspring group (10%)

narrative_results.statement: FFA levels during low-dose insulin clamp were inversely associated with S I(clamp) calculated during both low- and high-dose insulin clamp

evidence.quote: FFA levels during the low-dose insulin clamp (which is more appropriate to study the antilipolytic insulin action) were inversely associated with the S I(clamp) calculated during both the low- (r (cid:1) -0.57, P (cid:1) 0.033) and high-dose insulin clamp (r (cid:1) - 0.58, P (cid:1) 0.028)

narrative_results.statement: Log(I b) and log(FFA b) were significantly associated with IR in simple regression analysis, and were selected as best predictors in multiple stepwise regression

evidence.quote: Simple regression analysis showed that, when taken separately, log(I b) and log(FFA b) were significantly associated with IR, whereas log(G b) was not. The multiple stepwise regression analysis selected log(I b) and log(FFA b) as the best set of predictors of IR

narrative_results.statement: Incorporation of fasting FFA concentration into QUICKI improved its association with insulin sensitivity and enhanced its discriminatory power

evidence.quote: Incorporation of the fasting FFA concentration into QUICKI not only improved its association with insulin sensitivity but also enhanced its discriminatory power

figures_graphs.caption: FIG. 1. Correlation between SI(clamp) and QUICKI (left panel) and between SI(clamp) and revised QUICKI (right panel). Offspring of type 2 diabetic

evidence.quote: FIG. 1. Correlation between SI(clamp) and QUICKI (left panel) and between SI(clamp) and revised QUICKI (right panel). Offspring of type 2 diabetic

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Directly contradictory family history status: one states 'with family history' (n=17), the other states 'no family history' (n=40)
Contradictory descriptions of study population: 'without family history' vs 'offspring of type 2 diabetes' represent incompatible inclusion criteria

Pass 1 describes target plasma insulin concentration (350 pmol/liter) while Pass 2 describes target glucose concentration (5 mmol/liter). These are different parameters of the euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp.
Pass 1 describes target plasma glucose concentration (5 mmol/liter) while Pass 2 describes clamp duration (150 minutes). These are distinct parameters being conflated.
Pass 1 describes clamp duration (150 minutes) while Pass 2 describes blood sampling interval (15 minutes). These are different procedural parameters.
Pass 1 describes blood sampling frequency during clamp (15 minutes) while Pass 2 describes fasting period before study (10 hours). These are unrelated parameters.
Pass 1 describes fasting period before clamp (10 hours) while Pass 2 describes body composition assessment timing (2-3 days). These are completely different measurements.

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Log(I b) and log(FFA b) were significantly associated with IR in simple regression analysis [significant association]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: Incorporation of fasting FFA concentration into QUICKI improved its association with insulin sensitivity and enhanced its discriminatory power [improvement and enhancement]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: Incorporation of fasting FFA concentration into QUICKI improved its association with insulin sensitivity and enhanced discriminatory power [improvement and enhancement]

aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_1_summary: S I(clamp), M value, and GIR were inversely associated with fasting FFA [inverse association]
aligned_narrative_pairs.pass_2_summary: S I(clamp), M value, and GIR were inversely associated with fasting FFA [inverse association]
Multiple stepwise regression selected log(I b) and log(FFA b) as the best set of predictors of IR [best predictors]

semantic_groups.rationale: Directly contradictory family history status: one states 'with family history' (n=17), the other states 'no family history' (n=40)
Contradictory descriptions of study population: 'without family history' vs 'offspring of type 2 diabetes' represent incompatible inclusion criteria
Same fact (N=14 for dose-response study subgroup); pass_2 includes additional context about assay type and methodology
Identical inclusion criteria for age range (19-45 years)
Same fact with minor abbreviation difference: 'Body mass index' vs 'BMI', both specify <27 kg/m2
Identical inclusion criteria for race (white)
Identical body weight stability requirement (6-12 months); negligible formatting difference
Same measurement (insulin infusion rate 40 mU/(m2·min)); pass_2 includes 'Assay' category prefix and slightly different formatting

Pass 1 describes target plasma insulin concentration (350 pmol/liter) while Pass 2 describes target glucose concentration (5 mmol/liter). These are different parameters of the euglycemic hyperinsulinemic clamp.
Pass 1 describes target plasma glucose concentration (5 mmol/liter) while Pass 2 describes clamp duration (150 minutes). These are distinct parameters being conflated.
Pass 1 describes clamp duration (150 minutes) while Pass 2 describes blood sampling interval (15 minutes). These are different procedural parameters.
Pass 1 describes blood sampling frequency during clamp (15 minutes) while Pass 2 describes fasting period before study (10 hours). These are unrelated parameters.
Both describe the low dose insulin infusion rate in the dose-response study as 10 mU/(m2·min). Wording differs slightly but facts are identical.
Both describe the high dose insulin infusion rate in the dose-response study as 40 mU/(m2·min). Wording differs slightly but facts are identical.
Both describe the low dose target plasma insulin concentration as 150 pmol/liter in the dose-response study. Wording differs slightly but facts are identical.
Pass 1 describes fasting period before clamp (10 hours) while Pass 2 describes body composition assessment timing (2-3 days). These are completely different measurements.
Both describe a 3-week dietary preparation/pre-study diet and exercise period
Both specify days 3-8 of menstrual cycle for women study timing, only minor wording differences
Both describe plasma glucose measurement using glucose analyzer; pass_1 includes CV fasting value while pass_2 specifies Beckman Coulter brand, complementary information
Pass_1 contains CV clamp measurement data with no corresponding entry in pass_2
Both describe plasma insulin measurement via microparticle enzyme immunoassay; pass_1 includes CV fasting value while pass_2 specifies IMx brand, complementary information
Pass_1 contains CV clamp measurement data with no corresponding entry in pass_2
Pass_1 contains FFA fasting CV measurement with no corresponding entry in pass_2
Pass_1 contains FFA clamp CV measurement with no corresponding entry in pass_2
Both describe FFA sample storage at -70°C, identical values and meaning despite minor wording differences
Both describe FFA analysis timing of 0-5 working days, same fact with minor phrasing variation
Pass 1 contains information about steady-state clamp duration (30 minutes) but pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains information about steady-state clamp time window (120-150 minutes) but pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains information about steady-state clamp coefficient of variation threshold (<7%) but pass 2 is empty
Both report identical study population total N of 57
Pass 2 contains information about FFA sample collection tube specification but pass 1 is empty
Both describe the same finding about HOMA-IR association with SI(clamp) and FFA incorporation. Pass_1 includes 'the relationship' while Pass_2 omits it, but the core fact is identical.
Identical statements about revised QUICKI improving relationships with SI(clamp) and GIR.
Both convey the same fact about increased fasting plasma glucose and FFA in offspring. Pass_1 says 'increased in offspring group' while Pass_2 says 'increased in offspring' - negligible difference.
Identical facts about 21% and 20% reductions in GIR and SI(clamp). Only difference is comma placement before 'respectively' which is stylistic.
Both describe HOMA-IR and QUICKI not detecting differences but revised QUICKI finding reduction. Pass_1 specifies '10%' while Pass_2 omits the percentage, but the core finding is the same.
Both state FFA levels inversely associated with SI(clamp). Pass_1 specifies 'during both low- and high-dose insulin clamp' while Pass_2 omits this detail, but the main finding is equivalent.

Pass_1 includes that variables 'were selected as best predictors in multiple stepwise regression' while Pass_2 omits this. Both mention significant association in simple regression, but Pass_1 provides additional methodological detail.
Identical statements about FFA incorporation into QUICKI improving association and enhancing discriminatory power.
Both extractions contain identical text describing the inverse association between SI(clamp), M value, GIR and fasting FFA.
Pass 1 is empty while Pass 2 contains information about stepwise regression predictors of IR. This is an orphan entry present only in Pass 2.
